text: The surgeon places a liver retractor and opens the pars flaccida, excising the gastropathic ligament to improve visualization. Dissection of the common hepatic artery is initiated.
The surgeon dissects and excises the station eight A hepatic artery lymph nodes using a ligature device, with the lymphatics visibly taken and sent to pathology.

The robotic system facilitates precise dissection of the common hepatic artery and gastroduodenal artery. Additional station eight A lymph nodes are resected and retrieved using the robotic instruments.

The surgeon isolates the right gastric artery, clips it, and excises it using a ligature. Subsequently, the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) is dissected free, extending at least three centimeters in length.

The surgeon inserts a hepatic artery infusion catheter into the abdomen. A bulldog clamp is applied proximally on the gastroduodenal artery (GDA), which is then ligated distally using a 2-0 silk tie and a metal clip. An additional silk tie is placed proximally below the bulldog clamp but left untied. An arteriotomy is performed on the GDA, and the catheter is inserted and secured with the previously placed silk tie. Two more ties are added to ensure the catheter remains in place.

Methylene blue is injected into the hepatic artery infusion catheter, visibly confirming the distribution pathway for future chemotherapy to both lobes of the liver, with no leakage observed into the stomach or duodenum.
